content.text: Vendor escalation is the correct action when you have confirmed that the failure originates in the Anthropic Claude API — not in your own application code, infrastructure, network, or configuration. Escalating to Anthropic before ruling out internal causes wastes time: Anthropic support will ask for the same diagnostic information you should have already gathered, and the round-trip costs 2–4 hours of incident resolution time.

The decision to escalate externally is always made by the on-call team lead (Tier 3 in your escalation chain). The primary on-call engineer gathers evidence; the team lead decides when that evidence is sufficient to open a vendor ticket.

attrs.alt: Vendor escalation decision flow: verify internal infra health, check Anthropic status page, then open ticket at appropriate priority tier

content.text: Complete all of these before opening an Anthropic support ticket. Submitting a ticket without this information triggers a round-trip that delays triage by hours.
Confirm your own infrastructure health: load balancer healthy, application servers responding, no recent deployments in the last 4 hours, no configuration changes in the last 24 hours.
Confirm the issue is reproducible from a direct curl command to api.anthropic.com — not through your reverse proxy or API gateway.
Check the Anthropic status page at status.anthropic.com. If an incident is already posted and covers your symptoms, subscribe to updates rather than opening a duplicate ticket.
Identify the exact affected model(s): list every claude-* model version that exhibits the problem. Specify whether it is isolated to one model or affects all models.
Identify the exact affected endpoint(s): /v1/messages, /v1/messages/batches, etc.
Collect the x-request-id response header from at least 5 failed requests. This is Anthropic's internal request ID and allows them to find the request in their own logs instantly.
Collect a precise time range in UTC: when did the issue start? Is it ongoing or intermittent?
Estimate the blast radius: what percentage of your API calls are affected? How many users?

#!/bin/bash
# pre_escalation_diagnostic.sh — collect required info before opening Anthropic ticket

API_KEY="$ANTHROPIC_API_KEY"
MODEL="claude-sonnet-5"
ENDPOINT="https://api.anthropic.com/v1/messages"

echo "=== Pre-escalation diagnostic ==="
echo "Timestamp (UTC): $(date -u +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ)"
echo ""

echo "--- Direct API probe (bypassing your infra) ---"
for i in 1 2 3 4 5; do
    RESPONSE=$(curl -s -w "\n%{http_code}" -X POST "$ENDPOINT" \
      -H "x-api-key: $API_KEY" \
      -H "anthropic-version: 2023-06-01" \
      -H "content-type: application/json" \
      -d '{"model":"'"$MODEL"'","max_tokens":5,"messages":[{"role":"user","content":"ping"}]}')

    HTTP_CODE=$(echo "$RESPONSE" | tail -1)
    BODY=$(echo "$RESPONSE" | head -1)
    REQUEST_ID=$(curl -sI -X POST "$ENDPOINT" \
      -H "x-api-key: $API_KEY" \
      -H "anthropic-version: 2023-06-01" \
      -H "content-type: application/json" \
      -d '{"model":"'"$MODEL"'","max_tokens":5,"messages":[{"role":"user","content":"ping"}]}' \
      | grep -i x-request-id | tr -d '\r')

    echo "Probe $i: HTTP $HTTP_CODE | $REQUEST_ID"
    if [ "$HTTP_CODE" != "200" ]; then
        echo "  Error body: $(echo $BODY | python3 -c 'import sys,json; d=json.load(sys.stdin); print(d.get("error",{}))')"
    fi
done

echo ""
echo "--- Include the above output verbatim in your support ticket ---"

Anthropic support tiers correspond to the severity of the incident. Enterprise customers get access to a Technical Account Manager (TAM) and a dedicated Slack channel for P0 incidents; API-only customers use the support portal for all tiers.
Dedicated TAM Slack channel + support portal

If your service's production availability depends on Claude, an enterprise support contract with TAM access is not optional — the difference between a 15-minute and 1-hour initial response during a P0 outage is 45 minutes of your error budget burning.

An effective support ticket gives Anthropic engineers everything they need to correlate your incident with their internal telemetry. The ticket should be written by the primary on-call engineer — not delegated to someone who did not observe the failure directly.
claude-sonnet-5 /v1/messages returning 529 for 15% of requests since 14:32 UTC
Load balancer healthy, no deploys in 6h, direct curl from prod network also fails
No active incident posted as of 14:45 UTC
~2,000 users; checkout flow blocked for EU region

# support_ticket_template.py — generate a pre-filled support ticket body from probe data
from dataclasses import dataclass
from datetime import datetime, timezone

@dataclass
class SupportTicket:
    severity: str                  # P0, P1, P2
    affected_models: list[str]
    affected_endpoints: list[str]
    start_time_utc: datetime
    error_rate_pct: float
    http_status_codes: list[int]
    error_types: list[str]
    request_ids: list[str]         # x-request-id samples from failed requests
    anthropic_status_page: str     # "no incident" or "incident XXXX posted"
    business_impact: str

    def render(self) -> str:
        models = ", ".join(self.affected_models)
        endpoints = ", ".join(self.affected_endpoints)
        start = self.start_time_utc.strftime("%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ")
        codes = ", ".join(str(c) for c in self.http_status_codes)
        errors = ", ".join(self.error_types)
        req_ids = "\n  ".join(self.request_ids)
        return f"""
## Incident Report — {self.severity} — {start}

**Affected models:** {models}
**Affected endpoints:** {endpoints}
**Start time (UTC):** {start}
**Error rate:** {self.error_rate_pct:.1f}% of requests

**HTTP status codes:** {codes}
**Error types:** {errors}

**Sample x-request-id values (failed requests):**
  {req_ids}

**Anthropic status page at time of escalation:** {self.anthropic_status_page}
**Internal infrastructure health:** Confirmed healthy. Direct curl to api.anthropic.com from production network also fails.

**Business impact:** {self.business_impact}
"""

# Example
ticket = SupportTicket(
    severity="P1",
    affected_models=["claude-sonnet-5"],
    affected_endpoints=["/v1/messages"],
    start_time_utc=datetime(2024, 4, 15, 14, 32, tzinfo=timezone.utc),
    error_rate_pct=15.0,
    http_status_codes=[529],
    error_types=["overloaded_error"],
    request_ids=["req_01XYZ", "req_01ABC", "req_01DEF", "req_01GHI", "req_01JKL"],
    anthropic_status_page="No incident posted as of 14:45 UTC",
    business_impact="~2,000 users in EU region; checkout flow dependent on Claude classification step is blocked.",
)
print(ticket.render())

Once a ticket is open, the most important discipline is keeping your own internal incident management independent of Anthropic's response timeline. Waiting for Anthropic to respond before taking any mitigation action is a mistake — their response tells you whether the issue is on their side, but your mitigation options (fallback model, graceful degradation, circuit breaker) are available regardless.
Run two tracks simultaneously after opening a vendor ticket:

Internal mitigation track: attempt fallback to an alternative model, enable graceful degradation mode, or activate the circuit breaker to stop sending requests that are failing. These actions reduce user impact while Anthropic diagnoses.

Vendor communication track: monitor the ticket for response; provide any additional information Anthropic requests promptly (within 10 minutes during an active P0); escalate within Anthropic's system if the initial response SLA is exceeded.

# fallback_on_vendor_incident.py — activate fallback when vendor escalation is open
import anthropic
import os

PRIMARY_MODEL = "claude-sonnet-5"
FALLBACK_MODEL = "claude-haiku-4-5-20251001"  # lighter model; lower cost, faster, less capable

# Vendor incident flag — set True when Anthropic support ticket is open and P1+
VENDOR_INCIDENT_ACTIVE = os.getenv("VENDOR_INCIDENT_ACTIVE", "false").lower() == "true"

def call_claude(prompt: str, client: anthropic.Anthropic) -> str:
    """Call Claude with automatic fallback when vendor incident is active."""
    model = FALLBACK_MODEL if VENDOR_INCIDENT_ACTIVE else PRIMARY_MODEL

    try:
        resp = client.messages.create(
            model=model,
            max_tokens=1024,
            messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
        )
        return resp.content[0].text
    except anthropic.APIStatusError as e:
        if e.status_code in (529, 503) and not VENDOR_INCIDENT_ACTIVE:
            # Transient overload even without a declared incident — try fallback once
            resp = client.messages.create(
                model=FALLBACK_MODEL,
                max_tokens=1024,
                messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
            )
            return resp.content[0].text
        raise

If the initial response SLA is exceeded and the incident is still active, escalate within the ticket rather than opening a new one. Most enterprise support portals have an 'escalate to TAM' or 'request urgent review' action. Include the current time, how long it has been since the SLA target, and the current impact level.
P0 SEV1 ticket, no response after 15 minutes: click 'escalate' or message the TAM Slack channel directly.
P1 ticket, no response after 1 hour: use the portal's escalation button and add a comment with current impact.
P2 ticket, no response after 1 business day: send a follow-up comment with updated impact information.
Never open duplicate tickets for the same incident — it fragments the conversation history and delays resolution.
After the incident is resolved, whether by Anthropic or by your own mitigation, the vendor ticket must be properly closed and the findings incorporated into your runbooks.
Confirm that your own metrics have returned to baseline — do not close the ticket while your error rate is still elevated, even if Anthropic says the issue is resolved on their side.
Add a closing comment: when metrics normalized, what mitigation was in effect, and whether the fallback model or circuit breaker was involved.
Request a post-mortem summary from Anthropic if the incident was P0 or P1. Enterprise contracts typically include this.
Tag the ticket with the incident ID from your internal incident tracking system.
Add the specific error type and status code observed to the runbook's 'Anthropic API errors' section if it was not already present.
Document whether the fallback model behaved correctly and what quality impact it had.
Document any communication friction (information Anthropic requested that you did not have readily available) as a gap to close before the next incident.
Update the pre-escalation checklist if you discovered a step that would have identified the root cause faster.
Confirm metrics at baseline before closing ticket
Add closing comment with impact timeline
Update runbook with new error type / findings
Add vendor communication gaps to backlog

Vendor escalation to Anthropic is a last-resort action after internal root cause is eliminated, not a first response. The decision to escalate is made by Tier 3 (team lead), not the primary on-call. The escalation tier (P1, SEV1) determines the response SLA and contact channel — enterprise customers get TAM Slack access for P0 incidents; API-only customers use the support portal.
Pre-escalation checklist: verify internal infra health, reproduce from direct curl, check status.anthropic.com, collect x-request-id samples from ≥5 failed requests.
Support ticket must include: affected model versions, endpoints, start time UTC, error rate, HTTP status codes, x-request-id samples, and business impact.
Run internal mitigation (fallback model, circuit breaker) in parallel with the vendor communication track — do not wait for Anthropic to respond before taking action.
Escalate within the ticket if initial response SLA is exceeded; never open duplicate tickets.
After resolution: confirm metrics at baseline, close the ticket with an impact timeline, update runbooks with any new error types or communication gaps discovered.
